title: feat(preprocess): provider-pluggable wire feed ingestion (fetch_wire_documents) with dual-output WireDocument
author: keli-wen
body:
## Summary

Build a small, provider-pluggable OSS wire ingestion pipeline that fetches public feeds and returns replayable wire documents:

    async def fetch_wire_documents(config: WireFeedConfig) -> WireFetchResult: ...

The launch MVP supports PR Newswire. The adapter interface remains open for later providers without claiming unverified support for them.

## Motivation

QuantMind already has generic HTTP and HTML formatting primitives, and the preceding news/RSS work provides feed parsing and news normalization. The missing seam is a simple pipeline that composes those pieces for a wire provider whose public feed may expose a teaser instead of the full article.

The public API should keep provider-specific field mapping and body-source decisions behind an adapter while returning enough raw evidence to replay extraction when the cleaner improves.

## MVP design

### Provider adapter and config

Add a small `WireProvider` adapter interface and a built-in public PR Newswire configuration. PR Newswire follows the item link for the article body.

The common feed body mode remains available for custom feeds that explicitly guarantee full content. The feed config selects a provider, public feed URL(s), and the shared HTTP fetch policy.

GlobeNewswire and Business Wire are future adapters, not launch requirements for this MVP.

### Dual-output document

`WireDocument` carries:

- the raw feed-entry evidence;
- raw fetched article HTML when an article hop is used;
- fetch metadata needed to understand the evidence;
- cleaned Markdown body;
- provider, payload ID, canonical URL, title, publisher, UTC publication time, content hash, stable identity, and ticker hints.

Identity is derived from provider + payload ID + canonical URL rather than selecting only one identifier.

### Lightweight partial-failure result

A failed feed or article must not discard documents that were fetched successfully. `WireFetchResult` returns:

- successful documents;
- lightweight structured failures with provider, stage, source URL, item ID, error type, and message.

This result is intentionally local to the MVP. Shared batch hooks, monitoring, and metrics belong in a later generic batch-operation design.

### Shared HTTP robustness

Reuse the generic retry/backoff and per-host rate limiting from #97. Provider adapters must not implement their own copy of that infrastructure.

## Acceptance criteria

- A single async `fetch_wire_documents` entry point fetches and processes the current items returned by a configured public feed.
- A provider adapter interface and built-in PR Newswire config exist.
- Sanitized PR Newswire feed and article fixtures cover dual-output ingestion.
- Unit tests cover feed-body vs linked-article acquisition, duplicate payloads, partial extraction failures, raw evidence, cleaned Markdown, and stable identity.
- Failures for individual feeds/items are recorded while remaining items continue.
- A separate manual smoke checklist performs real PR Newswire feed and article fetches and reports PASS/FAIL per component.
- The normal unit-test/verify suite remains deterministic and does not require public network access.
- A simple runnable example demonstrates the public API.

## Non-goals for this MVP

- GlobeNewswire or Business Wire built-in adapters.
- Persistent cursors or watermarks.
- Pagination and time-window orchestration.
- Scheduling or durable storage.
- A shared batch-operation base class, hooks, monitoring, or metrics.
- Provider credentials, private endpoints, anti-bot systems, or production fallbacks.
- Database or downstream card-generation integration.

## Dependencies

- Depends on #96 for explicit feed-body vs linked-article acquisition.
- Depends on #97 for reusable HTTP retry/backoff and per-host rate limiting.

title: feat(fetch): HTTP fetcher robustness — retry/backoff + per-host rate limiting
author: keli-wen
body:
## ✨ Summary

`fetch_url` (`quantmind/preprocess/fetch/http.py`, lines 26–87) currently follows redirects, calls `raise_for_status`, applies a 30s timeout (line 29) and a 50MB body cap (line 30) — but has **no retry, no backoff, and no per-host rate limiting / politeness delay**. Propose making robustness available as an opt-in fetch-policy object while keeping the current one-shot API unchanged by default.

## 🎯 Motivation

The fetch helper is the shared entry point for both feed polling (`fetch_rss_feed`) and article-body fetches (`fetch_news_document`). For scheduled polling and burst article fetches, transient failures (5xx, request timeouts, connection resets) and unpoliteness (hammering a single host) are the norm rather than the exception.

The news design doc explicitly lists "rate limits" and "retries or fallbacks" as real acquisition concerns (`docs/design/en/news.md` §8, lines 418–419), but leaves them to per-adapter code. A shared, tunable primitive means every provider adapter does not have to re-implement backoff and rate limiting from scratch.

## 🔧 Proposed design

Add an optional `fetch_policy` (or similar) parameter to `fetch_url`:

- **Bounded retries** with exponential backoff + jitter for retryable errors (5xx, timeouts, connection resets).
- **Per-host concurrency / rate caps** (a politeness delay between requests to the same host).
- **Honor `Retry-After`** on 429 / 503 responses.
- **Terminal error classification**: clearly separate retryable failures from non-retryable ones (e.g. most 4xx), so callers get a stable signal instead of guessing.

Keep the API simple: with no policy passed, behavior is byte-for-byte the current single-shot GET.

## ✅ Acceptance criteria

- Retry/backoff unit tests with mocked transient failures (verify attempt counts, backoff schedule, and eventual success/give-up).
- Rate-limit behavior test (per-host cap / spacing).
- `Retry-After` honored in a test.
- **No behavior change** for the default single-shot GET unless a policy is passed.

## 🔗 References

- `quantmind/preprocess/fetch/http.py` — `fetch_url`, lines 26–87
- `docs/design/en/news.md` — §8, lines 418–419
- Unblocks #98 (provider-pluggable wire feed ingestion API)

title: bug: inline teaser suppresses full-article fetch in feed_item_to_news_document
author: keli-wen
body:
## Summary

`feed_item_to_news_document` currently fetches an item URL only when the cleaned feed body is completely empty. A non-empty RSS teaser therefore suppresses the full-article fetch and silently becomes the document body.

This is especially harmful for wire feeds that intentionally publish a short description and link to the full release.

## MVP design

Make the body acquisition source explicit:

    body_source: Literal["feed", "article"] = "feed"

- `feed` trusts the body carried by the RSS/Atom item and performs no article hop.
- `article` always follows `item.url` and extracts the article body.

Thread the setting through `feed_item_to_news_document` and `preprocess_feed_item`. Record whether the cleaned body came from the feed or fetched article in document metadata.

The default remains `feed`, preserving the current single-hop behavior. The built-in PR Newswire provider follows the article link because its public feed may contain teasers. The feed mode remains available for custom feeds that explicitly guarantee full content.

Automatic teaser heuristics are intentionally outside this MVP. Known providers should not depend on body-length guesses, ellipsis detection, or "read more" markers when their acquisition behavior is known.

## Acceptance criteria

- `body_source` is threaded through `feed_item_to_news_document` and `preprocess_feed_item`.
- `body_source="article"` follows the item URL even when the feed teaser is non-empty.
- `body_source="feed"` uses feed content without a second network request.
- Metadata records feed-vs-article body provenance.
- Missing article URLs and article extraction failures produce explicit errors.
- The default remains the existing single-hop feed behavior.
- Unit tests cover non-empty teaser, full feed content, missing URL, and extraction failure branches.

## Dependency

- Unblocks #98.

title: feat(preprocess): generic PageIndex — long-document tree build + reasoning-based query
author: keli-wen
body:
## ✨ Feature Summary

Add a small, general-purpose **PageIndex** utility: build a hierarchical page/section tree from any long document (PDF / HTML / markdown), and query it with reasoning-based tree traversal instead of vector search. Generic by design — papers, filings, transcripts, and reports all go through the same build/query path.

## 🎯 Motivation

Long financial documents (10-Ks, earnings-call transcripts, research papers, prospectuses) don't fit in one extraction pass, and flat chunk-based RAG loses document structure and page provenance. A page-anchored tree gives us:

1. **Structure-aware retrieval** — an agent (or flow) navigates section titles + summaries to the right nodes instead of embedding-similarity guessing.
2. **Page-anchored citations** — every extracted claim can reference `node_id` + page range, making citations verifiable against the source.
3. **One schema for all long documents** — not a paper-only feature; `Paper` is just the first consumer.

Prior art: [VectifyAI/PageIndex](https://github.com/VectifyAI/PageIndex) (MIT) validates the approach and node schema. We want our own minimal implementation aligned with QuantMind's knowledge shapes — not a wrapper over their cloud service.

## 📋 Detailed Description

Two capabilities, kept deliberately small:

**Build** — `document → PageIndexTree`

- Input: parsed document (reuse `preprocess.format` outputs: `pdf_to_markdown` / `html_to_markdown` with page metadata).
- Deterministic pass: heading/outline detection + page-range assignment (no LLM).
- Optional LLM pass: per-node `summary` generation (flow layer, standard Agents SDK usage).

**Query** — `(tree, question) → relevant nodes with page anchors`

- Reasoning-based traversal: an LLM sees the tree skeleton (titles + summaries), selects branches, descends until it returns node ids + page ranges.
- Plain async function, no retriever class hierarchy, no vector store dependency.

### Node schema (PageIndex-compatible)

```python
# quantmind/knowledge — aligned with TreeKnowledge
class PageIndexNode(...):
    node_id: str
    title: str
    start_index: int   # page range in source document
    end_index: int
    summary: str | None
    nodes: list["PageIndexNode"]
```

Whole-document trees remain `TreeKnowledge` artifacts; `Paper` becomes the first consumer but the schema stays document-type-agnostic.

## 🔧 Proposed Implementation

Respecting the import-linter contracts:

- `quantmind/preprocess/pageindex.py` — deterministic outline/page detection (preprocess stays LLM-free, depends only on `utils`).
- Node schema in `quantmind/knowledge/` (leaf).
- LLM summary pass + query traversal in `quantmind/flows/` (apex), using the Agents SDK directly.

### API Design

```python
from quantmind.preprocess import build_page_outline
from quantmind.flows import enrich_page_index, pageindex_query

outline = build_page_outline(markdown_pages)          # deterministic
tree = await enrich_page_index(outline, cfg=...)      # LLM summaries
hits = await pageindex_query(tree, "What does management say about capex?")
# -> [(node_id, title, (start, end))], page-anchored
```

## 🎨 User Experience

An agent working on a 300-page 10-K builds the tree once, then answers questions by traversal — each answer carries page anchors that a validator (or a human) can check against the source.

## 📊 Use Cases

1. **Papers**: section-aware extraction for `PaperKnowledgeCard`, citations with page ranges.
2. **Filings / transcripts**: navigate to "Risk Factors" / guidance discussion without embedding infrastructure.
3. **Benchmark golden targets**: page-anchored structured outputs are easier to grade objectively.

## 🔗 Related Issues

- Relates to #71 (Agents SDK migration — flows layer is the LLM home)

## Implementation Considerations

- Keep it a utility, not a framework: functions, frozen/pydantic types per repo conventions; no retriever abstractions, no vector store.
- PDF page metadata: `pdf_to_markdown` must expose page boundaries; extend it if needed rather than re-parsing.
- Quality ceiling on messy PDFs is accepted for v1 (standard parsing; no OCR dependency).

### Breaking Changes

- [ ] This feature would introduce breaking changes
- [x] This feature is backward compatible

### Dependencies

- [ ] Requires new dependencies
- [x] Uses existing dependencies only

---

**Checklist**

- [x] I have searched existing issues to avoid duplicates
- [x] I have provided a clear and detailed description
- [x] I have explained the motivation and use cases
- [x] I have considered the implementation approach
- [x] I have thought about potential breaking changes

title: chore: add contributor instructions and quantmind-dev skills
author: keli-wen
body:
## Feature Summary

Add a small, contributor-focused instruction layer for coding agents working on the QuantMind repository.

The repository-level `AGENTS.md` and `CLAUDE.md` should stay concise and route development tasks to a mirrored `quantmind-dev` skill. Deep guidance should cover commit format, PR format, component development, tests, examples, and verification.

This issue is only about contributing to the QuantMind codebase. It does not define an end-user workspace or a product workflow for generating pipelines.

## Motivation

The current contributor instructions are inconsistent:

- `AGENTS.md` contains only a few generic development rules.
- `CLAUDE.md` contains a large amount of architecture history, temporary repository state, and roadmap detail.
- `CONTRIBUTING.md` still references removed modules and development patterns.
- Commit, PR, component-development, test, example, and verification guidance is spread across multiple files.

Coding agents need one short entry point and task-specific instructions that match the current repository.

## Proposed Structure

```text
AGENTS.md
CLAUDE.md
.agents/skills/quantmind-dev/
├── SKILL.md
└── references/
    ├── commit.md
    ├── pull-request.md
    └── develop-components.md
.claude/skills/quantmind-dev/
├── SKILL.md
└── references/
    ├── commit.md
    ├── pull-request.md
    └── develop-components.md
```

The Agent and Claude skill implementations must provide equivalent behavior.

## Requirements

### 1. Repository instruction files

Keep `AGENTS.md` and `CLAUDE.md` thin and aligned. They should contain only:

- a short project and module map;
- the canonical setup and verification commands;
- stable repository-wide architecture constraints;
- the existing test and example requirements;
- communication conventions;
- routing rules for loading `quantmind-dev`.

Do not duplicate detailed workflows that belong in the skill references. Tool-specific notes may differ, but the core development rules must remain consistent.

### 2. `quantmind-dev` skill

Add a contributor-facing development skill mirrored under `.agents/skills/` and `.claude/skills/`.

Use progressive disclosure: `SKILL.md` selects the relevant workflow, and only the matching reference file is loaded.

The skill must cover:

#### Commit workflow

- Use English Conventional Commit messages.
- Use a meaningful scope when applicable, following the repository's existing history.
- Keep each commit focused on one logical change.
- Inspect the staged diff and verification result before committing.
- Do not bypass hooks unless the user explicitly authorizes it.

#### Pull request workflow

- Use an English Conventional Commit-style PR title.
- Write the PR body in English.
- Reference the related issue when one exists.
- Summarize the change and list the verification performed.
- Keep PRs small and focused.
- Align `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE.md` with the documented workflow if needed.

#### Component development workflow

Route guidance according to the component being changed, including:

- `quantmind/knowledge/`
- `quantmind/configs/`
- `quantmind/preprocess/`
- `quantmind/flows/`
- `quantmind/mind/`
- `quantmind/utils/`

The workflow should explain how to:

- find and follow the nearest existing implementation pattern;
- preserve the dependency boundaries enforced by import-linter;
- avoid meaningless wrapper methods and premature abstractions;
- add unit tests under the matching `tests/<module>/` path using `unittest.TestCase`;
- add one focused example under `examples/<module>/` for a new feature;
- mock external services and cover success and failure paths;
- update public exports and documentation when required;
- run targeted tests during development and `bash scripts/verify.sh` before handoff.

### 3. Contributor documentation alignment

Update `CONTRIBUTING.md` only as needed to remove stale architecture, commands, or component instructions and to point to the canonical development workflow.

Avoid maintaining the same detailed rule in multiple files.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `AGENTS.md` and `CLAUDE.md` provide the same core contributor guidance.
- [ ] Both instruction files are concise and route task-specific work to `quantmind-dev`.
- [ ] `quantmind-dev` exists under both `.agents/skills/` and `.claude/skills/`.
- [ ] Commit, PR, and component-development workflows are documented.
- [ ] Guidance matches the current module layout and `scripts/verify.sh`.
- [ ] New-feature guidance requires a unit test and a focused example.
- [ ] Stale contributor instructions are removed or corrected.
- [ ] No runtime code, CLI, configuration schema, or dependency is introduced.
- [ ] The full repository verification passes with `bash scripts/verify.sh`.

## Out of Scope

The following require separate product discussion and are intentionally excluded:

- any `workspace/` convention or reserved directory;
- gitignore policy for user-chosen local workspaces;
- nested Git repositories;
- workspace initialization commands;
- user-generated pipeline scaffolding;
- pipeline manifests or validators;
- product-facing skills such as `/new-pipeline` or `/fix-pipeline`;
- a new `quantmind` CLI;
- a new project-wide `PRINCIPLES.md` or product repositioning.

## Compatibility

- [x] Backward compatible
- [x] Uses existing dependencies only

title: paper_flow() fails on its own README example: dict-typed nodes field breaks strict structured output, UUID id fields reject the model's natural output
author: isorobo
body:
## Summary

The single-paper example in the README does not run against the currently resolved dependency versions (`openai-agents>=0.14`, no ceiling, resolves to `0.18.1`):

```python
import asyncio
from quantmind.configs import PaperFlowCfg
from quantmind.configs.paper import ArxivIdentifier
from quantmind.flows import paper_flow

async def main() -> None:
    paper = await paper_flow(
        ArxivIdentifier(id="2401.12345"),
        cfg=PaperFlowCfg(model="gpt-4o-mini"),
    )

asyncio.run(main())
```

Two separate bugs stack on top of each other.

## Bug 1 — `Agent(output_type=Paper)` raises before any LLM call

`TreeKnowledge.nodes` is `dict[UUID, TreeNode]` (`quantmind/knowledge/_tree.py`). OpenAI's strict-mode structured output — the default in current `openai-agents` releases — cannot represent dict-typed fields at all (arbitrary keys are incompatible with a fixed JSON Schema). `paper_flow` (`quantmind/flows/paper.py`) passes the bare `Paper` type straight into `Agent(output_type=out_type)`, with no `strict_json_schema=False` escape hatch, so the run fails immediately:

```
agents.exceptions.UserError: Strict JSON schema is enabled, but the output type is not valid.
Either make the output type strict, or wrap your type with AgentOutputSchema(YourType, strict_json_schema=False)
```

## Bug 2 — once strict mode is off, UUID-typed id fields reject the model's output

Wrapping the output type in `AgentOutputSchema(Paper, strict_json_schema=False)` gets past bug 1, but a non-strict schema drops the UUID format constraint. The extraction agent then does exactly what you'd expect an LLM to do when free-form: it fills `TreeNode.node_id` / `parent_id` / `children_ids` with readable slugs (`"root"`, `"introduction"`, `"methodology"`) and `BaseKnowledge.id` with the paper's arXiv id (`"2404.11584"`), rather than leaving these at their UUID4 defaults. Validation then fails with ~80+ `uuid_parsing` errors.

## Repro

Ran the unmodified README example against a real paper (arXiv `2404.11584`, "The landscape of emerging AI agent architectures for reasoning, planning, and tool calling: A survey", model `gpt-4o-mini`) on the current `main` (`8e21888`). Fails both ways described above. Full traceback available on request.

## Fix

Opened #<!--pr-number--> — changes `TreeNode.node_id`/`parent_id`/`children_ids`, `TreeKnowledge.root_node_id`/`nodes`, `BaseKnowledge.id`, and `PaperKnowledgeCard.paper_id` from `UUID` to `str` (accepts both UUIDs and readable slugs), and has `paper_flow` pass `AgentOutputSchema(out_type, strict_json_schema=False)` instead of a bare type. Verified: the unmodified README example now completes end-to-end with zero validation errors against the same real paper, and the full test suite passes (232/233 — the one failure is pre-existing and unrelated, a Windows path-separator assertion in a local-file test).

## Environment

- `openai-agents==0.18.1` (resolved from the unpinned `>=0.14` floor)
- Windows 11, Python 3.12.12

title: Golden Harness: verify loop with ruff + pytest + basedpyright + import-linter
author: keli-wen
body:
Part of #71.

## Motivation

As QuantMind migrates from a self-built agent runtime to a domain library on top of OpenAI Agents SDK (#71), every subsequent PR will introduce new modules (`knowledge/`, `configs/`, `preprocess/`, `flows/`, `mind/`). To keep the codebase disciplined under this rapid evolution, we need a reproducible verify loop that runs locally and in CI before any product PR proceeds.

## Scope

Add a single `scripts/verify.sh` that runs the following in fixed order, fast-failing on the first error:

1. **`ruff format --check`** — formatting must be clean
2. **`ruff check`** — lint must pass
3. **`basedpyright`** (or `mypy --strict`) — type check must pass
4. **`import-linter`** — architectural boundary contracts must hold (e.g., `knowledge/` cannot import from `flows/`; `preprocess/` is leaf-only)
5. **`pytest --cov`** with a coverage floor (start at 70%, ratchet up over time)

Configuration to land alongside:

- `pyproject.toml` — basedpyright (or mypy) section; tighten ruff config
- `[tool.importlinter]` (or `.importlinter`) with module boundary contracts derived from the target architecture
- `.pre-commit-config.yaml` — include type-check + import-linter in addition to existing ruff + pytest hooks
- `.github/workflows/verify.yml` — GitHub Actions workflow triggering on every PR, executing `scripts/verify.sh` exactly once

## Reference

Inspired by `llmquant-data-core`'s `scripts/verify.sh` pattern (`ruff format --check` → `ruff check` → `basedpyright` → `import-linter` → `pytest --cov`).

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `scripts/verify.sh` exists, is executable, and exits 0 on a clean checkout of master
- [ ] All five checks integrated and configured
- [ ] Pre-commit hooks include all five checks
- [ ] CI workflow triggers on every PR and matches `scripts/verify.sh` exactly
- [ ] `CLAUDE.md` updated to reference the verify loop as the canonical local check
- [ ] First import-linter contract codifies the dependency direction from the target architecture (`knowledge/` and `preprocess/` are leaves; `flows/` depends on both; `mind/` depends on `knowledge/`; etc.)

## Open Questions

- **basedpyright vs mypy**: basedpyright is faster and stricter; mypy is more conventional. Recommend basedpyright (matches `llmquant-data-core`).
- **Coverage floor starting value**: 70% is conservative; we'll ratchet up as new modules land with tests-first discipline.
- **CI compute budget**: is the existing GitHub Actions quota enough for the new workflow on every PR? Likely yes for a project this size, but worth verifying.

title: Tracking: Migrate Agent layer to OpenAI Agents SDK
author: keli-wen
body:
## Motivation

QuantMind invested ~3 months building a smolagents-derived Agent runtime in PRs #61, #65, and #67 (~1900 lines of `brain/` + `tools/` + supporting modules). We now believe this was a wrong direction:

- **Agent runtime is a commodity in 2026.** OpenAI Agents SDK, smolagents, LangGraph, etc. compete on the same surface. Maintaining our own gives QuantMind no differentiation.
- It consumes engineering time that should go into financial-domain work (data schemas, e2e pipelines, research-friendly memory patterns).

This issue tracks the migration to **OpenAI Agents SDK** as the underlying agent runtime, with QuantMind repositioning as a domain library on top.

## Target Architecture

```
quantmind/
├── flows/        # e2e processing pipelines (paper_flow, news_flow, ...)
├── knowledge/    # Pydantic-based knowledge schema standard (Paper, News, ...)
├── preprocess/   # fetch (arxiv/http/doi/local) + format (pdf/html/markdown)
├── mind/         # QuantMind's distinctive cognitive layer; mind/memory/ MVP
├── configs/      # centralized BaseFlowCfg + per-flow cfg + input types
├── magic.py      # resolve_magic_input: natural language → (input, cfg)
└── utils/        # logger only
```

Key principle: QuantMind does **NOT** rebuild Agent runtime, lifecycle hooks, tracing, multi-agent handoff, or tool framework. Those come from `openai-agents`.

## Roadmap

- [ ] **PR1** (#70): Clean removal of self-built agent runtime
- [ ] **PR2**: `knowledge/` + `configs/` skeleton (Pydantic schemas + centralized cfg)
- [ ] **PR3**: `preprocess/` two-layer structure (fetch + format)
- [ ] **PR4**: `flows/` + `paper_flow` + `batch_run` + `magic.py`; concurrent removal of `flow/` and `llm/`
- [ ] **PR5**: `mind/memory/filesystem` MVP + trajectory archive
- [ ] **PR6+**: Second flow (news / earnings) / observability cookbook / longer-term `KnowledgeStore`

## Archive

The pre-pivot agent runtime is preserved as a snapshot on the [`archive/agent-runtime-final`](https://github.com/LLMQuant/quant-mind/tree/archive/agent-runtime-final) branch on origin for historical reference. It will not be merged back to master.

## References

- OpenAI Agents SDK docs: https://openai.github.io/openai-agents-python/
- Lifecycle / RunHooks: https://openai.github.io/openai-agents-python/ref/lifecycle/
- MCP integration (filesystem server): https://openai.github.io/openai-agents-python/mcp/
- Tracing: https://openai.github.io/openai-agents-python/tracing/
- SDK announcement: https://openai.com/index/the-next-evolution-of-the-agents-sdk/

title: feat: Where is adaptive summarization part?
author: Yvette-0508
body:
Hi there,

I'm exploring the codebase referenced in the paper, but I can't seem to find the logic for the Adaptive Summarization step mentioned in the text.

Is this implemented in a submodule I might have missed, or is this feature intended to be released in a future update?

Thanks!

title: [Feature] brain: Brain Components Based on Memory & Tool (Inspired by smolagents)
author: keli-wen
body:
## ✨ Feature Summary

Implement a brain component system in QuantMind, inspired by memory and tool-based agentic architectures. Use `smolagents` from HuggingFace as a primary reference to guide the development of a lightweight agentic core.

## 🎯 Motivation

- Enable QuantMind to support agentic workflows by modularizing cognitive ("brain") components.
- Leverage the best practices from `smolagents`, adapting their lightweight and extensible approach.
- Facilitate further extensions for memory and tool integrations.

## 📋 Detailed Description

The goal is to create a flexible, composable system for brain components:
- Components should be inspired by agentic patterns (memory, tools, reasoning, etc.).
- Take `smolagents` (https://huggingface.co/spaces/smol-ai/smolagents) as an example for agentic patterns and extensibility.
- Provide interfaces for memory management, tool integration, and action planning.

## 🔧 Proposed Implementation

- Study the design and interfaces of `smolagents`.
- Define core abstractions (e.g., Brain, Memory, Tool, AgentStep).
- Develop a minimal working prototype that can be extended.
- Ensure components are lightweight and modular.

### API Design

```python
class Brain:
    def __init__(self, memory: Memory, tools: List[Tool]):
        ...
    def step(self, input: str) -> str:
        ...
```

### Configuration

```yaml
brain:
  memory: basic
  tools:
    - search
    - calculator
```

## 🎨 User Experience

- Developers can easily compose new agents by mixing and matching memory and tool modules.
- Clear documentation and examples provided.

## 📊 Use Cases

1. **Research agent**: Uses memory and search tools to answer questions.
2. **Trading assistant**: Combines memory and financial calculators for decision support.
3. **Conversation bot**: Employs memory to track dialogue and external tools for real-time info.

## 🔗 Related Issues

- Relates to # (add if known)
- Blocks # (add if known)
- Depends on # (add if known)

## Implementation Considerations

### Breaking Changes

- [ ] This feature would introduce breaking changes
- [ ] This feature is backward compatible

### Dependencies

- [ ] Requires new dependencies
- [ ] Uses existing dependencies only

---

**Checklist**

- [ ] I have searched existing issues to avoid duplicates
- [ ] I have provided a clear and detailed description
- [ ] I have explained the motivation and use cases
- [ ] I have considered the implementation approach
- [ ] I have thought about potential breaking changes

title: [Feature] flow: Agentic LLMFlow Middleware for Dynamic Tool-Driven Reasoning
author: keli-wen
body:
## ✨ Feature Summary

Introduce a new `LLMFlow` class: an agentic runtime that leverages LLMs for dynamic, tool-driven reasoning within QuantMind. This will allow the framework to autonomously plan and execute multi-step financial analysis tasks by orchestrating tools in response to user input, inspired by modern agent design patterns.

We will primarily take `smolagents` repo as base to develop our `LLMFlow`.

## 🎯 Motivation

The current `BaseFlow` supports only statically orchestrated task flows, limiting the framework's ability to respond adaptively to user goals or leverage external capabilities. By adding `LLMFlow`, QuantMind can:
- Dynamically select and use tools based on LLM-driven reasoning
- Support autonomous, multi-step task execution (e.g., query data, analyze, compare, summarize)
- Integrate tightly with the agentic ecosystem, including future third-party or open-source toolkits

## 📋 Detailed Description

`LLMFlow` will:
- Operate in an agentic loop, maintaining structured message history (not just a string scratchpad)
- Delegate action planning to the LLM, which returns either a final answer or a list of tool calls
- Execute tool calls asynchronously and append their results to the history for further reasoning
- Support configuration via `LLMFlowConfig`, specifying the reasoning LLM and the set of available tools (as `BaseTool` instances)

## 🔧 Proposed Implementation

- Add `LLMFlowConfig` (in `quantmind/config/flows.py`) to specify the reasoning LLM and available tools
- Implement `LLMFlow` (in `quantmind/flows/llm_flow.py`), following agent middleware patterns:
    - Asynchronous reasoning loop with structured message history
    - Tool call schema generation and execution via native LLM APIs
    - Clear error handling and extensibility for new tool types
- Provide usage and extension documentation

### API Design

```python
# quantmind/config/flows.py
class LLMFlowConfig(BaseFlowConfig):
    reasoning_llm_identifier: str
    tools: List[BaseTool]

# quantmind/flows/llm_flow.py
class LLMFlow(BaseFlow):
    async def run(self, initial_input: str, max_iterations: int = 5) -> Any:
        ...
```

### Configuration

```yaml
llmflow:
  reasoning_llm_identifier: "gpt-4-turbo"
  tools:
    - name: "company_financials"
    - name: "data_fetcher"
```

## 🎨 User Experience

Users can define a flow that reasons and acts using LLMs and a configurable set of tools. Complex tasks like “Summarize this report and compare key metrics with last quarter” become possible with minimal code changes.

## 📊 Use Cases

1. **Automated Report Analysis**: Fetch, analyze, and summarize financial reports using multiple tools in sequence, determined at runtime.
2. **Conversational Data Exploration**: Users ask natural language queries, and the agent dynamically selects tools to pull, process, and present data.
3. **Third-Party Tool Integration**: Easily plug in new tools (e.g., external APIs) for domain-specific analytics.

## 🔗 Related Issues

- Relates to #47 (BaseTool design and implementation)

## Implementation Considerations

### Breaking Changes
- [ ] This feature would introduce breaking changes
- [x] This feature is backward compatible

### Dependencies
- [ ] Requires new dependencies
- [x] Uses existing dependencies only

---

**Checklist**
- [x] I have searched existing issues to avoid duplicates
- [x] I have provided a clear and detailed description
- [x] I have explained the motivation and use cases
- [x] I have considered the implementation approach
- [x] I have thought about potential breaking changes

title: [Feature] Optimize unit test performance
author: keli-wen
body:
## ✨ Feature Summary

Unit tests in the current codebase are running significantly slower than expected (almost `1min`). This issue proposes to investigate and optimize the performance of the unit test suite.

## 🎯 Motivation

Slow unit tests hinder development velocity and CI/CD efficiency. Optimizing test execution time will improve developer productivity and provide quicker feedback during development and code review cycles.

## 📋 Detailed Description

- Identify bottlenecks in the current unit test suite.
- Profile and analyze test execution to find slow tests or inefficient patterns.
- Refactor or parallelize tests where possible.
- Review test data setup and teardown for unnecessary overhead.
- Consider using more efficient test frameworks or configurations.

## 🔧 Proposed Implementation

- Use profiling tools or verbose test runners to locate slow tests.
- Split large test cases into smaller, more focused tests.
- Implement parallel testing if supported by the framework.
- Optimize fixtures and mocks to minimize redundant computation.

## 🎨 User Experience

Developers and contributors will experience faster feedback from CI/CD pipelines and local test runs, leading to an improved development experience.

title: [Example] flow: Add more examples demonstrating flow customization
author: keli-wen
body:
## ✨ Feature Summary

Add more example code and documentation to demonstrate how to customize flows in QuantMind. This will help users better understand how to adapt and extend flows for their specific needs.

## 🎯 Motivation

Currently, users may find it challenging to adapt QuantMind’s flow system due to limited examples. By providing additional customization examples, we can lower the barrier for advanced usage and encourage community contributions.

## 📋 Detailed Description

Expand the documentation and codebase to include more concrete examples showing:
- How to create custom flows from scratch
- How to extend or override existing flows
- Real-world scenarios for advanced customizations
- Best practices for modular flow design

## 🔧 Proposed Implementation

- Update the official documentation site and/or in-code docstrings
- Provide at least 2–3 new example scripts or notebooks
- Add comments and explanations to each example

### API Design

```python
# Example of a custom flow class
class MyCustomFlow(BaseFlow):
    def run(self):
        # custom logic here
        pass
```

### Configuration

```yaml
# Example of custom configuration for a flow
my_custom_flow:
  param1: value1
  param2: value2
```

## 🎨 User Experience

Users will be able to reference concrete examples and templates, making it easier to customize flows for their use cases.

## 📊 Use Cases

1. **Use Case 1**: A user wants to implement a domain-specific flow logic.
2. **Use Case 2**: A developer wants to extend QuantMind with a new processing step in an existing flow.
3. **Use Case 3**: Community contributors want to reference best practices for advanced flow customization.

title: [Feature] tool: BaseTool design and implementation
author: keli-wen
body:
### Overview

As outlined in the roadmap, the next step for QuantMind is to support external Tools. To enable this, we need a core `BaseTool` component that defines the standard interface and behavior for all tools.

### Goals

- Define a `BaseTool` class/interface with:
  - Standard method signatures (e.g., `run`, `validate`, `serialize`)
  - Configuration options and default parameters
  - Error handling and logging strategies

- Provide a reference implementation in Python that:
  - Implements the `BaseTool` interface
  - Includes minimal working examples (e.g., a dummy tool)
  - Demonstrates integration with the existing framework

- Write tests to verify:
  - Method signatures and return types
  - Proper error handling on invalid input
  - Basic serialization/deserialization workflows

- Document usage:
  - How to extend `BaseTool` for new tools
  - Code examples in the README or docs directory

### Benefits

- Establishes a consistent foundation for all future tools
- Simplifies onboarding and development of new tool integrations
- Improves maintainability and test coverage of tool components

### Tasks

- [ ] Design interface and behavior in a design document or docstring
- [ ] Implement `BaseTool` in `quant_mind/tools/base.py`
- [ ] Add unit tests and user examples for core methods
- [x] Update documentation with usage examples

title: [Docs] Add developer tutorial for pre-commit and formal issue templates
author: keli-wen
body:
### Problem

The repository's README currently lacks a standard developer setup tutorial, specifically instructions on setting up and using pre-commit hooks. Additionally, there are no formal issue templates to guide contributors when opening bug reports or feature requests.

### Proposed Changes

- Add a **Dev Setup** section in the README with:
  - Steps to install and configure pre-commit hooks
  - Instructions for running hooks locally and in CI
  - Example commands and troubleshooting tips

- Introduce ISSUE_TEMPLATEs in `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/`:
  - `bug_report.md` for bug reporting
  - `feature_request.md` for feature suggestions
  - Include appropriate labels, checklists, and structured sections in each template

### Benefits

- Standardizes development workflows across the project
- Reduces onboarding friction for new contributors
- Ensures consistency and completeness in issue reports

title: [Bug] Fix the accidental creation of folders during unit testing
author: keli-wen
body:
### Description

Now, when we execute the following unit test:

```shell
bash scripts/unittest.sh
```

One of unit tests will unexpectedly create a folder named `test_data`. Although this does not affect the program's correct operation, I believe fixing it contributes to a good user experience.

title: [Example] Implement Quant Paper Agent as a Production-Level example
author: keli-wen
body:
### Description

As outlined in our project's `README.md`, a key item on our roadmap is to deliver a "First production level example (Quant Paper Agent)". This agent will serve as a concrete implementation of our **Stage 1: Knowledge Extraction** architecture, demonstrating an end-to-end workflow for transforming raw financial documents into structured knowledge.

The goal is to create a pipeline that:
1.  Fetches research papers from arXiv.
2.  Parses their content.
3.  Tags them with relevant financial topics.
4.  Generates a summary.
5.  Saves the final output as a standardized `Knowledge` object.

This issue tracks the tasks required to build the initial version of the Quant Paper Agent.

### Tasks

To build the agent, we need to chain together several existing and new components into a cohesive flow:

-   [ ] **1. Connect `ArxivSource`**: Utilize the existing `ArxivSource` to search and retrieve papers based on user-defined queries (e.g., "machine learning finance").
-   [ ] **2. Integrate `LlamaParser`**: Process the raw paper files (PDFs) obtained from `ArxivSource`. This will act as the "Intelligent Parser" in our architecture to extract the core text and data.
-   [ ] **3. Implement `LLMTagger`**: Use an LLM-based tagger to automatically categorize the parsed content. The agent should be able to assign relevant topics and research areas, fulfilling the "Tagger" role in the architecture.
-   [ ] **4. Create a `SummaryFlow`**: Implement a flow that takes the parsed content and generates a concise summary. This will be a key part of the "Workflow/Agent" orchestration.
-   [ ] **5. Generate Standardized `Knowledge`**: Consolidate all the outputs—metadata from `ArxivSource`, parsed text, tags, and the summary—into a single, structured `Knowledge` object.
-   [ ] **6. Persist Knowledge**: Save the generated `Knowledge` object to a local or remote store using our storage abstraction (e.g., `LocalStorage`).
